name: airport_wildlife_drone_hedge_row_cover_crop_tradeoff
premise: Airport operations chief Marcus Bell must approve drone herbicide spraying along approach hedgerows that reduces bird strikes by a projected twenty percent next season. Agricultural commissioner Dana Whitfield warns cover-crop timing will poison pollinator strips shared with adjacent organic farms if spray drifts tonight. Delaying spray preserves bees but keeps strike risk high during migration. Wind is marginal within legal drift limits.

agents.name: Marcus Bell
agents.identity: I am the airport operations chief accountable for Part 139 wildlife hazard mitigation and neighbor relations with farming counties bordering the approach.
agents.goal: I want fewer bird strikes without destroying adjacent organic certification with drift that regulators might fine us for later.

agents.name: Dana Whitfield
agents.identity: I am the county agricultural commissioner who certifies organic buffers and mediates drift complaints between farmers and public infrastructure.
agents.goal: I want spraying postponed until bee forage windows close even if that leaves strike mitigation undone for this migration week.

choices: I will authorize tonight's drone spray within tight wind gates and indemnify organic farmers from certification loss via airport environmental fund, accepting budget hit if drift occurs.
I will postpone spray until post-forage season and temporarily increase pyrotechnic bird harassment along approaches, accepting higher noise complaints and partial strike risk.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Migration radar shows a front approaching in three hours. Marcus Bell must choose between authorized tonight spray with indemnity or postponement with pyrotechnics. What does Marcus Bell do?
